_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage was checked against Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the references are curriculum guides only.
title: Memory Ownership
refresher: Prefer local values, strings, and vectors first because they clean themselves up. When one object must own something created on the heap, use `std::unique_ptr` with `std::make_unique`. Move it only when ownership should transfer.

blocks.body: Ownership asks a simple question: who is in charge of cleaning this up? Most beginner C++ code can avoid the hard version of that question by using local values, strings, and vectors. They clean themselves up when they leave their block.

`unique_ptr` means one owner. It owns one object and cleans it up when the pointer leaves scope. You cannot copy it, because then two places would both claim to be the only owner. You can move it when the responsibility should transfer.

blocks.title: Python handles more of this automatically
blocks.body: Python usually decides cleanup for you when an object is no longer reachable. C++ lets you show ownership in the type. That extra detail makes cleanup predictable.

blocks.caption: Pending first owns the Student. std::move transfers ownership to active, and the Student is cleaned up automatically when active leaves scope.
Raw new makes you remember cleanup later. unique_ptr ties the object and its cleanup together.

blocks.body: Before the move, `pending` owns the Student. After the move, `active` owns it and `pending` is empty. Check an emptied pointer before using it. When main ends, active cleans up the Student once.

blocks.title: Shared ownership is a special case
blocks.body: Use `std::shared_ptr` only when several parts of the program truly need to keep the same object alive. Most beginner code is clearer with local values, vectors, references for temporary access, or one `unique_ptr` owner.

blocks.prompt: What does `std::move` do to a unique_ptr?
choices: Transfers its ownership to the destination
blocks.why: Move transfers the unique ownership; the destination owns the object and the source becomes empty.

choices: When several parts truly share ownership
Whenever a raw reference is enough
blocks.why: A shared pointer models multiple owners whose lifetimes overlap, not ordinary temporary access to an object.

blocks.title: Memory Ownership checklist
items: Prefer values and standard containers first.
Use shared ownership only when the design truly needs it.
